speaker: interviewer
text: And this is the ninth tape of Barbara Traub's interview. So I believe you're in Munich.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am in Munich and I live with my cousin and her husband, who had a very nice-- I mean, very nice-- they somehow got an apartment from the American authorities, I imagine. And I began working for the AJDC, for the American Joint Distribution Committee. I was in the transportation department. My boss was Dr. Orenstein, a very, very nice person. The chaplain was Rabbi Shapiro. I remember very well. The place was on-- in Munich, not the [GERMAN]. That was-- that was the HIAS. Well, I don't remember anymore the exact address. But I worked there, and I got rations. And we are now in '47, '48. [SIGHS] And then comes the question of go-- of coming to America. And what happened was that the Romanian quota was not good. It was a quota sys-- system, but in 1947, they brought in the McCarran Act, by which America would accept 100,000 war refugees. Well, I was among them, but I think I have to tell you-- clearly, this I almost forgot, and I shouldn't. I got married. That's why I came to America because he had here a brother. I wanted to go to Israel where my brother was. I got married to some second or third cousin of mine who, at the time, when I was living with my cousin in Munich, he was also in Munich. And he was working for the HIAS. And the people working for the HIAS wore uniforms, and they were considered more or less as occupation personnel with some minor distinction, clearly. He had a very good job. He was much older than I was, and I remembered him as a distant relative who now and then used to come to my parents' house. I never had much to do with him. He was much older than I. At one time or another when he would see me, he would greet me very respectfully. You know, I kiss your hand, miss, that sort of a thing, which was the appropriate thing. I was a little girl, and he was a big man, an old man. Well, he wasn't really old, but from my point of view. And when he heard that I came to Munich and I stayed with my cousin, he came one evening loaded with bananas. I'd never seen a banana before. With Coca-Cola-- I'd never seen-- I didn't even know what Coca-Cola was. I refused to drink it at first. And he came with a pair of tickets to the opera house, which was half-bombed in Munich, but he managed those tickets. And he was very, very giving and very protective of me, and I always considered him as an older uncle. And he took me to places and, and made things very pleasant for me while I was working at the Joint. And he was very good to me. And then one day, he said to me, he proposed to me marriage. And he said, quote, I will be as good to you as your mother and father were, unquote. And I accepted because that's all I wanted then. I remember saying to myself, well, my big romance, I had it in my life. I am-- my life is over and he's-- all I want, again, is my mother and father, and here was a man who offered me that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I continued working at the Joint when this immigration thing came in, and he had a brother here. His name was-- because he died many years ago. His name was Martin Stauber, S-T-A-U-B-E-R, which is my mother's maiden name because they were somehow related.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came over-- oh, yes and this is what he said to me. He said, look, I promise you I have to see my brother, my one and only brother. But if you will want, after that, we'll go to Israel. Well, we came over here, and little by little, things we're not working out between us. There was not only the age difference. There was also a tremendous cultural difference. I didn't speak English at all when I came to this country. I wanted to go to school but I didn't. I don't remember-- yes, I know why I didn't. I didn't want to go to night school and somehow, I felt that-- I don't know why I had the resistance to night school. Instead, I went practically every day to movies and began reading books. Well, went to movies, to French movies where there were subtitles because at that time, I was speaking French still fairly fluently, so I could make the translation. And then I began reading books that I read either in Hungarian or in Romanian. I began reading them in English. Thomas Mann I remember very well reading. And little by little, I sort of picked up the language.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I wanted to work, to-- I wanted-- I wasn't happy. And I went to-- what can I do? What can I do? We weren't in a position-- my then husband was not in a position to, to-- I didn't speak English that well to even imagine that I would dare apply to college or something like that. So I did the next best thing. I took a course, of all the things, in shorthand in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: But somehow, I learned it. And since I always wanted to be a lawyer, I got a job with a patent lawyer. I had no idea what patent law was. The only word I knew was law, and I worked there. Eventually, I did hold a very nice position there, and I learned a great deal about patent law. And eventually, I even started to take courses in patent law with the idea of becoming a patent agent, although I didn't particularly like it because it was-- it was very technical. But meanwhile, the marriage didn't quite work out because, as I said, a combination of the age difference and the cultural difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say, the cultural difference, what do you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: I should be perhaps more specific. I should say, the educa-- you know, I, I, I wanted other things. I wanted to study. I wanted something. I just couldn't see myself being a secretary, even though it was a very fine position because I was in charge of several. But it wasn't my way of life, and he was playing gin rummy with his friends. And he was a very good tie salesperson.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a sort of a, I should say, not so much the educational life-- the level of culture, the level of-- how should I express it? He was a simple man, a very good man. He was very good to me. There is no question about it, but something was lacking. Something was lacking.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to go to the theater. I wanted to, to, to go to the opera. I wanted to learn. I mean, the first American play I saw was I remember it very well with Claude Rains, and it was by Koestler, originally the Hungarian playwright. And, and, and he just-- it didn't gel. And after two years, I applied and got-- much to his chagrin because he begged me not to-- I did get an annulment. And now, I could really do what I wanted to do, and I began taking all kinds of courses with Margaret Mead at the New School. And I was pursuing now to become a patent agent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a good girl from--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, I wasn't quite a good girl because I really married Martin because he said he's going to be as good to me as mother and father. But I didn't realize that I was not a child anymore. And only late-- as we came to this country, I think America had a lot to do with it. I realized that I'm a woman. By that time, I was 22 years old and this is not the atmosphere that I can see ending my, my years living this way, with him playing gin rummy and, and, and having some silly conversations with people that-- there was no content. There was no intellectual content to our lives. And it's easy for me to put it in these terms, but at that time, I did not realize that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other women your age doing what you were doing? Were you unique in your ambition? I think-- I think m-- you see, most of my friends were his friends. And they were ha-- very happy just, you know, having children and being housewives, which was nothing wrong in that. But I just felt I was not finished. I was unfinished. And I-- I'm thinking about your grandmother.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never thought about her but yes. Yes. She did something which went against the grain of the times or the temper of the times at then. And so I did get annulment, and it was very sad. But he eventually married and found a very nice lady, I understand, and, and he died some time ago. Now, as far as I am concerned, I began taking courses. You know, I was just like a sponge taking it all in. And I was dating. But I also had to support myself, so I had to have that job, which was a nice job-- nice job. I mean, in those days, I don't know, I was earning $200 a week, something like that. And then, one nice day, I met Herman B. Traub as I was coming out from the New School after a-- because I was taking courses with actually Ashley Montagu and Margaret Mead because one was a physical anthropologist. The other one was a cultural anthropologist. All right. And it was a blind date, which I didn't want to make, but this girlfriend of mine insisted. And you know, just see him once. You don't have to see him again. And that's when I met Herman B. Traub on, on 12th Street near the New School. And six months later, we got married. And this was a different life for me. It was an American life. You have to understand that until then, I was mostly in the European milieu, and we talked mostly Hungarian. And I married him, and I always said to Herman that eventually, I want to go back to college. And he said, fine, but just relax right now, which I did. And it was very nice. And after three years or so, I said, OK, now I want to go back to college. And then, he said, I don't think you should because you know, you-- I, I want a house maker. And now, I was a good girl. I wish I would have been my grandmother. And I began to do what was expected of me, you know, being the wife of a fairly successful attorney, socially and so forth. Nonetheless, I did get very much involved in Jewish affairs. Starting with the synagogue, I was the president of this or that. Eventually, I became a very important person in Hadassah. And then came Ralph in '6-- I got married in 1957, and Ralph came in 1963, at which point there was no way that I would go back to school. And this is when I became involved in Jewish affairs. And I kept being involved in Jewish affairs until 1976, which was around Ralph's bar mitzvah-- my son's bar mitzvah-- which was one of the happiest days in my life when I saw him being bar mitzvahed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What in particular made that a happy day?

speaker: interviewee
text: That Rabbi Nedicz, who was the rabbi at the time at the Park Avenue Synagogue, said something which I wanted him to say, which I asked him to say, and he said it before the full congregation. And he said, you see, Ralph, the fact that you are now bar mitzvahed means that those bad people, the Nazis, did not win. You are here, and you are a Jew. That made it a very meaningful and happy day in my life. And that was in 1976.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, I said to my husband, Herman, like it or not, I am going back to college. And I was not a good girl because he said, why? You are so busy with this, and you are so busy with that. And, you know, they want you to be president of, of the Hadassah chapter in New York. But no.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I matriculated at Sarah Lawrence. They had continuing education courses. I got two years of college because although I didn't have any papers to prove that I had a baccalaureate diploma, my name was found in a book written by the chief rabbi of Kolozsvar, who listed all the students in the last grade. And my name was there, and so they gave me two years And the 60 credits that I had to take for the next two years, I think I made it in six months. And but then, something happened at Sarah Lawrence. I met a writer by the name of Saul Yurick, Y-U-R-I-C-K-- not Saul, Sal, S-A-L. And I started to write short stories, which was part of the course I was taking, and I decided I really want to write. And I gave up the law. However, when I went to Sarah Lawrence, the idea was that I'm going to get my degree and then go to law school. And once Sarah Lawrence accepted me, I went to-- I had an interview with the dean of the law-- of law school at Columbia, where-- of which my husband is a graduate.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told him, I've been accepted and I would like to go to law school. What are my chances because I am not a young woman, et cetera, et cetera? So he said, all that matters are grades. So fine, I said. But what happened at Sarah Lawrence? I s-- somehow, I began to write, and I gave up law, the idea of being a lawyer. And I'm sure much to my husband's relief. [LAUGHS] And that's what I am doing since. I am very, very involved in this particular project that Steven Spielberg is sponsoring because the importance of the testimony is the more [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] needed now when the deniers are abounding all around who say it never existed. That six million Jews, if they died, it was heart attack, malnutrition, high blood pressure, but that gas chambers? It's made up. It's the Jews' fantasy.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is what is happening today, and I am very, very involved in, in making sure that the truth comes out. I write on this. I speak on this. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that's where we are at.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think your thirst for knowledge is so strong?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I think it's beautiful to, to know, to understand because I do think that ignorance breeds prejudice. Ignorance breeds bias. And only education inside perhaps may, may help to overcome this. And I also think-- I, I, I I think that without knowledge, without, without reading a poem that really moves you, without reading the or without-- I mean, without listening to music that moves you, life is drab. Life is drab without-- it's not--

speaker: interviewee
text: There, there is a book, the title of which is And I have no idea who wrote it because I remember one of the first books when I began reading English. This is what it is. Why did the cavemen paint those cave paintings in the Lascaux caves? Yes, perhaps there were some-- there was some religious significance, but they were made pretty. I saw those, those cave drawings before they closed the caves.

speaker: interviewee
text: There is something that the soul needs besides the food, the shelter. And what the soul needs is love. What the soul needs is friendship, goodness. And what the soul needs is the need to soar, sometimes, by just hearing, hearing a song. I mean, that's why.

speaker: interviewer
text: Aside from the work that you do on this project, is there-- is there something you would want to say to the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the way, I am also published author of a book called which does have to do a lot about the people of Sighet who came back. But it is a historical novel based-- drawn on, on factual things. And I did a book on the partisan resistance, as I said.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you say the title of the book and the date it was published?

speaker: interviewee
text: is out of print now. It was published in '79. And the book on the partisan-- the Jewish partisan resistance is called It has not yet been published. Well, what I would like to say is this. More or less, what I told the Pope last year when on April 7, as you may know, the Vatican for the first time in 50 years had a commemorative concert in the Vatican. And among the many, many 7,000 or so dignitaries, bishops, cardinals, et cetera, 100 survivors were also invited by the Vatican, and I was among them. And the Pope was very gracious in giving a private interview to the 100 of us. And each one-- and he spoke very movingly to us, saying something about how good it is to dwell in brotherhood in this house, which was a quote. We know that. And how terrible it was what had happened. I mean, he said the right things. And then every survivor went up and said something to the Pope. And when my turn came, I said to him that I am grateful to him for doing what he did by recognizing and commemorating this event because they, they-- there are those who deny that it ever happened. And that means that not only were-- was I a victim once, but I was vict-- I am victimized a second time by denying my first victimization. And that by his making it so worldwide, he does something to help put a nail in the coffin of the deniers. And for that, I wanted to thank him, and I did. And this is what I want to say, that forgetting or trivializing the Holocaust is a sure mark, a sure sign of repeating it. I also want to say that comparing atrocities-- comparing the Gulag, comparing other atrocities, terrible atrocities-- to the Holocaust is wrong. The Holocaust was unique-- unique in its scope, unique in its genocide, genocidal power, force, evil, which was directed only against Jews. If all those six million Jews would have said, we convert, and we'll become rabid Nazis, it wouldn't have helped. It was a relentless extermination of Jews, and this is why the Holocaust is unique. And I am very pained when it is compared to anything else. I would also like to my son, my hopefully grandchildren or anybody who listens to this tape, to understand the difference between a death camp and a concentration camp. Jews were not slated for concentration camps. A concentration camp was a picnic. Or if you consider that the Japanese were put in a concentration camp-- a terrible thing in this country-- and to say that I was in a concentration camp, it's a mockery. It's a trivialization of the truth because a concentration camp in Nazi Germany-- and there were many concentration camps. I'm not saying there were none. But being in a concentration camp was like being on the edge of the abyss, whereas being in something like Auschwitz, which was an extermination camp-- and so was Majdanek, Treblinka, et cetera, et cetera-- was being at the bottom of the abyss. And the only reason I got out was because the war was over when it was over. And I guess what I am saying is this. That if-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] if one is not one's brother's keeper, and you multiply that by hundreds and thousands, if one is not one's brother's keeper, then mankind can and did become murderous. And this must never happen again. That's why I am doing what I am doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
